response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
    "opinion": "Dealing with conversations on topics like healthcare and COVID vaccines requires a thoughtful, informed approach. As someone who's been through life experiences without formal job training or advanced schooling beyond what I managed personally, my perspective comes from living it rather than studying it extensively in an academic sense. My dad had his doctorate degree while my mom just earned her bachelor's degree – they're both very smart people but we never really sat down as a family to discuss these kinds of complex matters together. Now that I'm widowed and raising two kids on my own, plus being unemployed most of the time living here in Los Angeles... well let me tell you about this.\n\nFirst off, when it comes to managing discussions around COVID vaccines or general healthcare issues today – whether online through social media where I spend maybe an hour every day scrolling by or face-to-face with friends and family – my biggest advice is always preparation. You need facts on hand because emotions run high especially during times like pandemics. Since I'm not a professional in medicine myself, relying too much on what others say without checking for accuracy isn't smart.\n\nI think being open to different views while keeping an objective mindset helps build better dialogue around these topics. It's important though that everyone involved respects each other’s right to their own beliefs especially considering my Christian faith might differ from someone else's perspective even if we're discussing the same thing.\n",
    "reasoning_summary": "Based on my background as a middle-aged man with lower scores in openness, conscientiousness and extraversion but high agreeableness – I believe that managing discussions requires empathy yet factual grounding. Given my limited formal education compared to my parents' advanced degrees, I rely more on practical life experience than academic knowledge.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
Dealing with conversations on topics like healthcare and COVID vaccines requires a thoughtful, informed approach. As someone who's been through life experiences without formal job training or advanced schooling beyond what I managed personally, my perspective comes from living it rather than studying it extensively in an academic sense. My dad had his doctorate degree while my mom just earned her bachelor's degree – they're both very smart people but we never really sat down as a family to discuss these kinds of complex matters together. Now that I'm widowed and raising two kids on my own, plus being unemployed most of the time living here in Los Angeles... well let me tell you about this.

First off, when it comes to managing discussions around COVID vaccines or general healthcare issues today – whether online through social media where I spend maybe an hour every day scrolling by or face-to-face with friends and family – my biggest advice is always preparation. You need facts on hand because emotions run high especially during times like pandemics. Since I'm not a professional in medicine myself, relying too much on what others say without checking for accuracy isn't smart.

I think being open to different views while keeping an objective mindset helps build better dialogue around these topics. It's important though that everyone involved respects each other’s right to their own beliefs especially considering my Christian faith might differ from someone else's perspective even if we're discussing the same thing.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on my background as a middle-aged man with lower scores in openness, conscientiousness and extraversion but high agreeableness – I believe that managing discussions requires empathy yet factual grounding. Given my limited formal education compared to my parents' advanced degrees, I rely more on practical life experience than academic knowledge.
